title: Run Gemma 4 Locally with LM Studio's New CLI
excerpt: LM Studio's headless CLI now exposes Gemma 4 as an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint, letting you build a local coding agent with zero cloud costs and complete data privacy. Setup takes 10-20 minutes.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
TL;DR

LM Studio's headless CLI lets you run Google's Gemma 4 as a local API endpoint in about 10 minutes. Connect it to Claude Code or Cursor, and you get unlimited coding assistance with zero cloud costs and complete data privacy.

You're burning through your Claude API budget. Maybe you've hit that $200/month ceiling. Or your company's security team just rejected another cloud vendor. Either way, you need capable coding assistance that doesn't touch external servers or drain your wallet per token. The problem is that local AI setup sounds like something requiring Docker, custom API servers, and hours of configuration.

It doesn't anymore. LM Studio changed the equation with a headless CLI that actually works.

Why this matters right now

Cloud API pricing for developers has become genuinely expensive. A power user running dozens of coding sessions daily can easily spend $200-300 monthly on Claude Code or GitHub Copilot Pro. That's software engineer salary territory for many junior developers.

But cost is only part of the picture. Three concrete problems push developers toward local inference:

  • Per-token billing creates real friction for experimentation and iteration
  • Proprietary code flowing through cloud infrastructure violates compliance requirements at many companies
  • Rate limits and queuing add unpredictable latency during focused coding sessions

Until now, solving these problems meant wrestling with incomplete tooling. Today, LM Studio makes the whole approach practical enough that developers on Hacker News are sharing working setups without disclaimers.

Understanding Gemma 4 as your local option

Google released Gemma 4 in early 2026 as an open-weight model family. This means the weights exist publicly. You can download them, run them on your hardware, and never ping Google's servers. That's fundamentally different from Claude or GPT-4o, which only exist on company infrastructure.

Gemma 4 comes in four sizes. Your hardware determines which one makes sense:

Model Parameters Minimum RAM Real-world use case
Gemma 4 1B 1 billion 4GB Older laptops, embedded systems
Gemma 4 4B 4 billion 8GB Basic coding assistance on resource-limited machines
Gemma 4 12B 12 billion 16GB M1/M2 Macs, everyday coding tasks
Gemma 4 27B 27 billion 32GB+ M3 Pro and higher, demanding projects

On an M4 Max with 48GB, the 27B variant generates 30-40 tokens per second. That's genuinely responsive. The 12B version on an M3 Air handles autocomplete, code review, and refactoring without noticeable lag. Google's training optimizations squeeze more capability out of these parameter counts than you'd normally expect.

The honest assessment: Gemma 4 is solid for routine coding work. If 70% of your queries are straightforward tasks (write this function, explain this pattern, find that bug), local Gemma handles them well. The remaining 30% might still need cloud API access for deeper architectural reasoning.

The model doesn't matter. Whether you run it locally or cloud, the outcome depends on whether you're asking it a problem it can solve.

The headless CLI setup changes everything

LM Studio previously existed as a desktop GUI. You'd launch it, load a model, manually configure other tools to connect. The headless CLI strips away the interface and runs as a clean background server.

The key architectural detail: it exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. This matters because Cursor, Goose, Claude Code, and most modern coding tools can connect to any OpenAI-compatible backend. Once your local model runs as an API, these tools use it transparently, identical to pointing at real OpenAI or Anthropic servers.

You're not retrofitting incompatible integrations. You're using the exact same interface, just redirecting to localhost instead of api.openai.com.

Getting Gemma 4 running locally: step-by-step

  1. Download LM Studio from lmstudio.ai. Open the app, search for "Gemma 4", and download the variant matching your hardware. The 12B model is roughly 7GB compressed. Plan accordingly for your connection speed.
  2. Start the headless server with this command: 
    lms server start --model gemma-4-12b
    The model launches as an OpenAI-compatible API at http://localhost:1234/v1. Token generation speed logs to your terminal, useful for verifying performance hasn't degraded.
  3. Configure your coding tool. Set these environment variables before launching Claude Code or Cursor: 
    export ANTHROPIC_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:1234/v1
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=local
    LM Studio doesn't require actual API key authentication locally. Any value works for the key.
  4. Launch your coding assistant. The interface is identical to the cloud version. You're just using a different backend underneath.
  5. Verify the connection by sending a test prompt. If you see tokens generating at your logged speed, the setup is working correctly.

Total setup time: 10 minutes if you're comfortable with the terminal, 20-30 minutes if not. No Docker. No custom configuration files. No scripting.

Performance on actual work

Speed depends entirely on your hardware. An M4 Max with 48GB runs the 27B model fast enough that you forget it's local. An M3 Air with 16GB runs the 12B model fine, but you notice the latency compared to cloud API calls.

More important than raw tokens per second: what problems can Gemma actually solve?

Gemma 4 performs confidently on:

  • Generating functions from docstrings
  • Explaining code passages
  • Spotting obvious refactor opportunities
  • Writing unit tests
  • Catching syntax errors

Gemma 4 struggles with:

  • Architectural reasoning across large codebases
  • Unfamiliar frameworks or libraries
  • Problems requiring extensive context
  • Complex multi-step refactors

70-80%

reduction in cloud API costs using hybrid local/cloud approach

The practical approach: route routine queries through local Gemma, reserve cloud API access for problems demanding stronger reasoning. You'll cut cloud spend dramatically while maintaining quality where it matters.

Data privacy as the secondary benefit

Everything stays on your machine. Prompts never hit company servers. Proprietary code doesn't flow through cloud infrastructure. For developers working under data residency requirements or handling genuinely sensitive systems, this moves from nice-to-have to essential.

This explains why Goose gained traction with enterprise teams. It was built from the ground up to support local backends, meaning you get autonomous coding agents without cloud exposure.

Important

Local inference requires minimum 16GB RAM for realistic coding work. 32GB is comfortable for the 27B model. Anything less than 16GB limits you to the 4B variant, which handles basic tasks only.

Other models worth testing locally

Gemma isn't your only option. LM Studio runs several alternatives equally well.

Mistral Small 3 delivers excellent multilingual support with strong coding performance at 22B parameters. It runs comfortably on 32GB machines. DeepSeek Coder V3 was purpose-built for coding tasks and matches Gemma's benchmarks with a slightly smaller footprint. Microsoft's Phi-4 is a 14B model that performs above its parameter count on reasoning tasks and runs remarkably fast on consumer hardware.

LM Studio handles all of them with identical setup. The best model depends on testing against your actual codebase and hardware. Benchmark results matter far less than real performance on code you care about.

Local vs. cloud: when to use which

Run local inference if you're hitting cloud API cost ceilings, working with proprietary code under regulatory constraints, or need completely predictable latency. The tradeoff is model capability. Local models don't yet match Claude Opus or GPT-4o on genuinely difficult reasoning problems.

Stay cloud if you code occasionally, need state-of-the-art reasoning, or lack hardware meeting the 16GB minimum. A single month of Claude usage might cost less than the laptop upgrade required to run 27B models comfortably.

The practical answer for most developers: both. Route straightforward tasks through local Gemma. Keep cloud API access for hard problems. LM Studio makes that hybrid approach straightforward.

Quick Verification Checklist

  • LM Studio server is running and logging token generation speed
  • Environment variables ANTHROPIC_API_BASE_URL and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY are set
  • Claude Code or Cursor connects without authentication errors
  • First test prompt generates tokens at the speed LM Studio reported
  • Local model response quality is acceptable for your routine coding tasks
  • CPU and RAM usage are stable during extended coding sessions
